title: Budget delay costs millions in stock gains
summary: Budget delay costs millions in stock gains By ROBERT KOWALSKI Daily News Juneau Bureau JUNEAU - While the Legislature is struggling to cut as much as $40 million from next year's budget, the state may already have lost nearly that much in stock market earnings because lawmakers have no long-term budget plan. State revenue officials, concerned about low oil revenues and the uncertainty of the Legislature's enacting a multiyear budget proposal this year, have pulled half a billion dollars in investments out of the stock market. The decision, which they said was necessary to assure the state government has enough ready cash to offset budget shortfalls, has cost the state about $30 million in lost earnings in the booming market, according to Revenue Department estimates. "We're sitting here wo…

title: Old pad new digs for DFYS
summary: Old pad new digs for DFYS MacKay annex will house state agency By TOM BELL Daily News reporter Nearly 20 years after it left, the state of Alaska is returning to the MacKay annex, the empty two-story building at 323 E. Fourth Ave. The annex is next door to its more famous neighbor, the long-derelict 14-story MacKay tower. The state plans to house the Division of Family and Youth Services in the annex by Sept. 15. The division will move out of its current location, a three-story building at Eighth Avenue and F Street. Loretta Delk, a contracting officer with the Department of Administration, said the state has given notice that it chose Anchorage developer Marc Marlow's proposal to house the division in the MacKay annex. Three other groups had submitted proposals for different sites. Those …

title: Copyright © 1999 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: The ties that bind Restraint devices for elderly pose tough choice: freedom or safety? By LISA DEMER Daily News reporter An 81-year-old man, wobbly after a bout of pneumonia, struggled to get up on his feet. It was lunchtime, but a soft, cloth band around his waist restrained him in the chair in the community area of the Anchorage Pioneers' Home. An aide walking another resident to the dining room told the man to wait just a minute. Soon, Glen Rhodes, who once ran his own sawmill at Bird Creek, was released from his bonds and free to walk. He scuffed across the large room, the aide by his side steadying him. Rhodes' wife, Doris, fearful he might otherwise fall and break a hip, said of the soft restraints: "They are a godsend." Her sentiment mirrors that of many who have relatives in Alaska…

title: Midtown activist takes on Meyer
summary: Midtown activist takes on Meyer By PETER PORCO Daily News reporter In the race for Anchorage Assembly Seat G in Midtown, voters face a choice between the low-key, well-liked incumbent and a passionate community activist making her first bid for elected office. Kevin Meyer, running for his third term, presents formidable strengths as a politician. Challenger Michele Czajkowski brings to the race a keener-than-average knowledge of the Assembly and frustration over how ordinary citizens fare against city government. The election is April 20. Meyer is a 42-year-old purchaser for Arco Alaska who says good things have happened during his six years in office: Unemployment is low, crime is dropping, city schools are receiving more state money, young people have a skateboard park and traffic proble…

title: State cites care homes as unsafe
summary: State cites care homes as unsafe By LISA DEMER Daily News reporter The state's Division of Senior Services issued an emergency notice of violation Friday, saying that residents of three Anchorage homes for the elderly are in imminent danger because of insufficient and poorly trained staff. The notice directs the Golden Years homes to hire more staff and make other improvements by next week. The homes are owned by Richard Collins, who told licensing officials he has been having financial problems and has been unable to retain staff. Some staff members said they were not being paid. In a telephone interview Friday evening, Collins said that he planned to address the state's concerns and hire more workers. "As long as licensing wants me to do it, I'll do it," Collins said. The Golden Years ho…

title: Proposition 10 splits city
summary: Proposition 10 splits city Sides debate benefits of runoffs By TOM BELL Daily News reporter Proposition 10 - which would require city candidates to receive a majority of the votes cast to win an election - could fundamentally change city politics, say the measure's supporters and opponents. They disagree, however, on how. Conservatives would stand a better chance at winning, some say. Others say it would help candidates advocating extreme positions and hurt those with moderate ones. Some warn the measure could cause minorities to have a harder time gaining public office. Bob Allen, the Spenard businessman who led the petition drive that put the measure on the ballot, said the proposition would benefit the most qualified candidates because voters would be better informed. In a runoff electi…

title: Nader urges blocking BP-Arco merger
summary: Nader urges blocking BP-Arco merger By ROBERT KOWALSKI Daily News Juneau Bureau BP Amoco, Arco (Atlantic Richfield), and Alaska State Legislature web sites See related pages: http://www.bpamoco.com/ http://www.arco.com/ http://www.legis.state.ak.us/ JUNEAU - Consumer advocate Ralph Nader says BP Amoco's purchase of Atlantic Richfield Co. would violate federal and Alaska antitrust laws and should be blocked. The $25.6 billion acquisition, announced last week, would give the resulting firm too much control over North Slope oil resources and the trans-Alaska oil pipeline, said Nader, whose Washington, D.C., group, the Center for Study of Responsive Law, has challenged other large mergers. "There are shades of gray in mergers. This isn't even a shade of gray. This is as bright (an) anti-compet…

title: 'Challenge' turns survival lesson
summary: 'Challenge' turns survival lesson Three tell of unreported slide By S.J. KOMARNITSKY Daily News Reporter Avalanche web sites See related pages: http://www.caic.state.co.us/ http://www.csac.org/ http://www.avalanche.org/ As six snowmachiners at Turnagain Pass were being swept to their deaths by a mile-wide wall of snow, three other riders were fighting for their lives in an avalanche above Anchorage in Chugach State Park. While the former made headlines nationwide, the latter has been known only to the handful who witnessed it and the few they have told. In both cases, the avalanche was triggered by snowmachiners riding high on a steep slope warmed by hours of sunshine. Those at Powerline Pass survived to tell their stories through pure luck. "I knew it was dangerous," said Troy Feller, an …

title: Knowles team studies merger
summary: Knowles team studies merger By ROBERT KOWALSKI Daily News Juneau Bureau BP Amoco, Arco, and Alaska State Legislature web sites See related pages: http://www.bpamoco.com/ http://www.arco.com/ http://www.legis.state.ak.us/ JUNEAU - Gov. Tony Knowles on Thursday directed a team of his top legal, financial and resource officials to review BP Amoco's $25.6 billion purchase of Atlantic Richfield Co. and its impacts on the state jobs, competition and revenues. One of the officials, Attorney General Bruce Botelho, termed the buyout "the major single change in the oil industry" since construction of the trans-Alaska oil pipeline concluded in 1977. "It is a dramatic event. ... It raises significant concerns. It also raises potential opportunities." As the administration was gearing up to respond to …

title: Address upgrade makes air base more navigable
summary: Address upgrade makes air base more navigable By DON HUNTER Daily News reporter A half-century of haphazard street naming and numbering on Elmendorf Air Force Base will be replaced next month with a brand new scheme that should make it easier to navigate the sprawling air base. Lt. Richard Johnson, a base spokesman, said the new names and numbers will go up on April 30 at a materials cost of about $31,600. Johnson said the base ordinarily replaces about a third of its street signs every summer because of weathering and the occasional wayward snowplow, anyway. The change will require another $36,000 in labor costs, but those civilian and military personnel would be on the payroll anyway, he said. According to Johnson and a press release issued by Elmendorf Wednesday, the existing base stree…

title: Merger sets off alarms
summary: Merger sets off alarms Whitaker fears BP colonialism By ROBERT KOWALSKI Daily News Juneau Bureau BP Amoco, Arco, Alyeska Pipeline Service Company, Alaska State Legislature web sites See related pages: http://www.bpamoco.com/ http://www.arco.com/ http://www.alyeskapipe.com/ http://www.legis.state.ak.us/ JUNEAU - Alaska would become a colony of BP Amoco and would be in for an "epic battle" if the company succeeds in taking over Atlantic Richfield Co., the chairman of a legislative committee that oversees oil and gas issues said Tuesday. Alaska should force BP and Arco to divest all their North Slope natural gas holdings and give up a significant interest in the trans-Alaska pipeline as a condition of the acquisition, said Rep. Jim Whitaker, R-Fairbanks. If the companies don't agree to those …

title: ENGLISH: Judge says law unclear, puts debut on hold
summary: ENGLISH: Judge says law unclear, puts debut on hold By SHEILA TOOMEY Daily News reporter DILLINGHAM - The law requiring state and city officials to conduct government business only in English is unclear and appears to threaten free-speech rights, so it will not go into effect today as planned, a judge ruled here Wednesday. At the request of two groups of challengers, including teachers, a ferry worker and elected officials from several Native villages, Superior Court Judge Fred Torrisi issued a preliminary injunction. The order delays implementation of the law until the legal issues in the two pending lawsuits are hashed out. "Freedom of speech is a fundamental right which lies at the foundation of our system of government," Torrisi wrote. "If a law restricts speech, it must meet a high st…

title: Developer makes bid for Palmer creamery
summary: Developer makes bid for Palmer creamery By S.J. KOMARNITSKY Daily News Mat-Su Bureau PALMER - In its heyday in the 1940s and 1950s, the Matanuska Maid creamery in Palmer supplied half of the state's milk and housed a cannery, shopping center and produce exchange. Local children flocked to the T-shaped wooden building each evening to scoop out the sweet remnants from ice cream containers. In later years, the creamery housed a hardware store that sold everything from chicken feed to tractor tires and wood stoves. But since the store closed in 1985, this former town hub has become a town eyesore, a vacant and deteriorating structure on an untended plot in the middle of downtown Palmer. Now an Anchorage developer hopes to restore some of the historic building's old glory for a new use as offic…

title: Strike forcing changes
summary: Strike forcing changes By LISA DEMER Daily News reporter Lisa Goodwin fought back tears Thursday as she spoke of her 12-day-old daughter in the neonatal intensive care unit at Providence Alaska Medical Center. A situation that would wrench any parent cut even deeper for Goodwin with the nurse's strike. With many unfamiliar faces among the nurses, Goodwin said her stress and fear have escalated. Her baby, Fetina, was born with a cleft palate, cleft lip and Down's syndrome and weighs just 5 pounds. She requires specialized feeding care that only the most skilled nurses can provide, her mother said. "I know the nurses are the lifeline of the hospital," said Goodwin, who stepped outside to smoke a Marlboro, then another, something she said she has been doing too much of lately. Hospital admini…

title: THE STRIKE AT A GLANCE
summary: THE STRIKE AT A GLANCE * Hundreds of nurses at Providence Alaska Medical Center in Anchorage began a strike Thursday over failure to reach contact terms on pay and patient-care issues. * Hospital temporarily shuts down or scales back some departments - heart surgery, chest trauma, gastrointestinal unit, endoscopy unit and catheterization. * About 40 nurses cross picket lines for their regular shifts. Hospital also hires 160 temporary nurses. * State health officials say Providence so far continues to provide adequate care. * No new contract talks scheduled. title: Teenager dies in prank
summary: Teenager dies in prank Joke turns tragic when camper, fearing bear, kills friend By RACHEL D'ORO Daily News reporter A 17-year-old Anchorage boy died early Sunday after he was shot in the dark by a friend who mistook him for a bear near Johnson Pass Trail on the Kenai Peninsula, Alaska State Troopers said. Michael J. Callaway and another boy were trying to scare a group of friends when the shooting occurred about 1:30 a.m. on an old mining road near the trail head, said trooper Sgt. Mike Marrs. Callaway was struck in the upper leg and apparently bled to death while his friends drove him back to Anchorage 60 miles away, Marrs said. Callaway was pronounced dead at Providence Alaska Medical Center. Troopers were calling the shooting an accident, but said their investigation is continuing. Mar…

title: Search comes up empty
summary: Search comes up empty Troopers suspend hunt for missing Knik woman By RACHEL D'ORO Daily News reporter Alaska State Troopers called off an urgent hunt for a missing Knik woman Sunday after four days of scouring rough terrain near the Matanuska Glacier and a harrowing close call for two searchers in a trooper helicopter. Tracking dogs and at least 70 people looked for Susan Beth Crawford in the foothills of Talkeetna Mountains but found no sign of the 46-year-old woman, Trooper Sgt. Matt Leveque said. Troopers and volunteers searched dense woods on four-wheelers and on foot, as well as from the air. Crawford, who was reported missing Tuesday, left her home Aug. 8. Friends said she had been acting strangely before that. Troopers announced they were suspending the hunt after thick fog and blo…

title: Fire ordeal isn't over yet
summary: Fire ordeal isn't over yet By ELIZABETH MANNING Daily News reporter A blaze that killed a man, burned a woman and destroyed six units at the Beachfront Apartment complex has left fear and anger in its wake. Some of the families displaced by Thursday's fire scrambled Saturday to find new homes. Others who remained in their apartments at 5160 Taku Drive said they were worried someone would set the Beachfront ablaze again. "Everyone is scared to be here," said Frona Sullivan, 36, who lives in an apartment in the same complex as the scorched six-unit building. Anchorage Police won't comment on who started the fire or exactly how it happened. On Friday, they determined that someone intentionally set the 4:44 a.m. blaze and are investigating the death of a man who died in the fire as a homicide.…
